entry.title: End-to-End Automatic Order to Billing Process in SAP SD (Low-Coding Approach)
entry.content:
In line with the customer requirement, an automatic process was designed covering delivery creation, goods issue posting, and billing immediately after sales order creation.

The solution was implemented based entirely on standard SAP SD principles and designed under a low-coding approach, minimizing custom developments while preserving full control over the process.

1. Excluding Specific Customers from the Automatic Process

During the analysis phase, it became necessary to exclude certain customers from the automated flow.

To achieve this:

  • A custom indicator field was defined under Business Partner – SD (FLCU01) view.
  • Using transaction CMOD, field KDGRP4 was renamed as “Automatic Billing Exclusion”.
  • For customers that must remain outside the automated process, value “X” is maintained via customizing.

This design ensures flexibility and allows business users to control participation in the automated process without additional development.

2. Sales Document Configuration (VOV8)

Within VOV8 sales document type configuration, the relevant document types were adjusted by activating the Immediate Delivery setting.

This configuration enables the system to automatically trigger delivery creation directly from the sales order.

3. Dynamic Control in MV45AFZZ (SAVE_DOCUMENT_PREPARE)

At enhancement point MV45AFZZ – SAVE_DOCUMENT_PREPARE, a class-based method was implemented instead of writing logic directly into the include.

The method dynamically reads Business Partner data and manipulates field TVAK-LISOF depending on defined conditions.

Key Logic Summary:

  • If the document creator is not the workflow user (SAP_WFRT), automation is cancelled.
  • KNVV-KVGR4 is checked for the exclusion flag.
  • If the customer is marked with “X”, the automation flag (TVAK-LISOF) is cleared dynamically.
  • Otherwise, the process continues automatically.

This approach ensures that:

  • Standard configuration remains intact.
  • Automation can be centrally controlled.
  • Sales document level settings can be programmatically overridden when necessary.

  

METHOD zsd_save_document_prepare_1.

* inserted by serdar ulgen. 19.01.2026 11:01:55 .

  i_knvv-kunnr = i_vbak-kunnr.
  i_knvv-vkorg = i_vbak-vkorg.
  i_knvv-vtweg = i_vbak-vtweg.
  i_knvv-spart = i_vbak-spart.

  FIELD-SYMBOLS <lv_lisof> TYPE any.
  ASSIGN ('(SAPMV45A)TVAK-LISOF') TO <lv_lisof>.
  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    RETURN.
  ENDIF.

  IF i_vbak-ernam <> 'SAP_WFRT'.
    CLEAR <lv_lisof>.
    RETURN.
  ENDIF.

  SELECT SINGLE kvgr4
    FROM knvv
    INTO @i_knvv-kvgr4
    WHERE kunnr = @i_knvv-kunnr
      AND vkorg = @i_knvv-vkorg
      AND vtweg = @i_knvv-vtweg
      AND spart = @i_knvv-spart.

  IF i_knvv-kvgr4 = 'X' AND <lv_lisof> = 'A'.
    CLEAR <lv_lisof>.
  ENDIF.

* if u any question please contact with me.

 

4. Automating Goods Issue and Billing

After completing the Sales → Delivery flow, the next phase covered:

  • Goods Issue posting
  • Billing creation

Alternative Approach (Not Preferred)

An alternative solution could involve sequential BAPI calls for goods issue and billing within a custom program.

Although technically feasible, this method requires careful handling of:

  • ENQUEUE / DEQUEUE locks
  • Document synchronization
  • LUW management

Improper lock handling may lead to document conflicts.

 5. Leveraging Standard Output Type REA0

In this implementation, we leveraged the fact that standard SAP output type REA0 can directly trigger billing.

The approach:

  1. The standard program used in REA0 was copied.
  2. A Goods Issue BAPI call was added at the beginning.
  3. Billing creation was kept as standard.
  4. Output determination was assigned correctly to the relevant delivery types using VV21.

This step is critical — without correct output assignment, the automation will not execute.

6. Technical Highlights

  • Goods issue is triggered via WS_DELIVERY_UPDATE_2
  • Billing is created via /SPE/CREATE_NEW_BILLING_CALL
  • Error handling is managed through NAST protocol updates
  • Separate LUW handling is ensured via /SPE/CALL_PROC_IN_NEW_LUW
  • Minimal custom coding
  • Maximum reuse of SAP standard structures
REPORT  zsd_***_****.

TABLES: nast.
CONSTANTS: c_allready_billed TYPE fkstk VALUE 'C'.
DATA: gs_nast            TYPE nast,
      lt_delivery_data   TYPE leshp_delivery_t,
      ls_delivery_data   TYPE LINE OF leshp_delivery_t,
      lt_delivery_items  TYPE leshp_delivery_item_t,
      ls_doc_pos_ver_req TYPE /spe/doc_pos_ver_req,
      lt_doc_pos_ver_req TYPE /spe/doc_pos_ver_req_t,
      lv_dummy_message   TYPE bapiret2-message,
      lt_bapiret2        TYPE bapiret2_t,
      ls_bapiret2        TYPE bapiret2,
      ls_leshp_data      TYPE leshp_data.

FIELD-SYMBOLS: <ls_billing_req> TYPE LINE OF leshp_delivery_item_t.

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  ENTRY
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       create new bill
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
FORM entry USING returncode  TYPE sy-subrc
                 use_screen  TYPE c.

  DATA: ls_vbeln_vl TYPE vbeln_vl,
        lv_leave    TYPE c,                                 "n_1238590
        lv_error    TYPE c,
        ls_vbkok    TYPE vbkok,
        lt_prot     TYPE TABLE OF prott,
        lv_text     TYPE t100-text,
        lv_delivery TYPE vbeln_vl.

  FIELD-SYMBOLS: <ls_return> TYPE bapiret2.

  IF sy-uname = 'SAP_WFRT'.

  lv_delivery = nast-objky.
  ls_vbkok-vbeln_vl  = lv_delivery.
  ls_vbkok-wadat_ist = sy-datum.
  ls_vbkok-wabuc     = abap_true.

  CALL FUNCTION 'WS_DELIVERY_UPDATE_2'
    EXPORTING
      synchron      = abap_true
      vbkok_wa      = ls_vbkok
      delivery      = lv_delivery
    TABLES
      prot          = lt_prot
    EXCEPTIONS
      error_message = 1
      OTHERS        = 2.

  LOOP AT lt_prot ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<error>).
    IF <error>-msgty = 'E' OR <error>-msgty = 'A'.

      SELECT SINGLE text FROM t100 INTO @LV_text WHERE sprsl = @SY-langu AND arbgb = @<error>-msgid AND msgnr = @<error>-msgno.

      IF sy-subrc = 0 AND lv_text IS NOT INITIAL.
        MESSAGE lv_text TYPE 'E'.
      ELSE.
        MESSAGE ID <error>-msgid TYPE 'E' NUMBER <error>-msgno.
      ENDIF.

      returncode = 1.
      RETURN.
    ENDIF.
  ENDLOOP.

ENDIF.


* Redirect this NAST processing to separate LUW - in case it is
* called with dispatching time 4 (immediate processing)
* during update task                                        "v_n_1238590
CALL FUNCTION '/SPE/CALL_PROC_IN_NEW_LUW'
  EXPORTING
    is_nast  = nast
  IMPORTING
    ef_leave = lv_leave.
IF NOT lv_leave IS INITIAL.
*   Per default we set the status on error, so that it can be reprocessed
*   in error processing (RSNAST0F) if there is any syntax error / update
*   termination / ... in the decoupled NAST processing.
  returncode = 4.
  RETURN.
ENDIF.                                                    "^_n_1238590

returncode = 0.
* Get Item Data of Delivery
*  ls_leshp_data-item = 'X'.                                  "n_1822545
* Get Status of Outbound Delivery
ls_leshp_data-head_status = 'X'.
* Copy Nast Entry
gs_nast = nast.
ls_vbeln_vl = nast-objky.

CALL FUNCTION 'LE_DELIVERY_GET_BUFFERED'
  EXPORTING
    if_vbeln               = ls_vbeln_vl
    is_data                = ls_leshp_data
  CHANGING
    cx_deliveries          = lt_delivery_data
  EXCEPTIONS
    no_item_selected       = 1
    selected_item_enqueued = 2
    no_key_specified       = 3
    OTHERS                 = 4.
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  MESSAGE e267(/spe/vl) WITH ls_vbeln_vl INTO lv_dummy_message.
  returncode = sy-subrc.
ELSE.

* only one delivery can be found by premisses (VBELN).
  READ TABLE lt_delivery_data INDEX 1 INTO ls_delivery_data.
  IF sy-subrc = 0.
    IF ls_delivery_data-head_status-fkstk = c_allready_billed.
* Allready billed.
      returncode = 0.
      MESSAGE i265(/spe/vl) WITH ls_vbeln_vl INTO lv_dummy_message.
    ELSE.
*        lt_delivery_items = ls_delivery_data-item.         "v_n_1822545
*        LOOP AT lt_delivery_items ASSIGNING <ls_billing_req>.
*          MOVE-CORRESPONDING <ls_billing_req> TO ls_doc_pos_ver_req.
*          APPEND ls_doc_pos_ver_req TO lt_doc_pos_ver_req.
*        ENDLOOP.
      ls_doc_pos_ver_req-vbeln = ls_delivery_data-head-vbeln.
      APPEND ls_doc_pos_ver_req TO lt_doc_pos_ver_req.    "^_n_1822545

      CALL FUNCTION '/SPE/CREATE_NEW_BILLING_CALL'
        EXPORTING
          it_billing_req     = lt_doc_pos_ver_req
          iv_version_checked = 'X'
        IMPORTING
          et_return          = lt_bapiret2.
      LOOP AT lt_bapiret2 ASSIGNING <ls_return>.
        IF <ls_return>-type = 'E'.
          lv_error = 'X'.
        ENDIF.
      ENDLOOP.
      IF lv_error = 'X'.
        returncode = 1.
        use_screen = ' '.
      ELSE.
        returncode = 0.
      ENDIF.
    ENDIF.

  ELSE.
* No Entry found !!!
    MESSAGE e267(/spe/vl) WITH ls_vbeln_vl INTO lv_dummy_message.
    returncode = 9.
  ENDIF.
ENDIF.
CALL FUNCTION 'NAST_PROTOCOL_INITIALIZE'.
LOOP AT lt_bapiret2 ASSIGNING <ls_return>.
  IF <ls_return>-type = 'E'.
    lv_error = 'X'.
  ENDIF.
  PERFORM protocol_update_return USING <ls_return>.
ENDLOOP.
IF lv_error = 'X'.
  returncode = 1.
  use_screen = ' '.
ELSE.
  returncode = 0.
ENDIF.

IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  PERFORM protocol_update.
ENDIF.
ENDFORM.                    " ENTRY

*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       FORM PROTOCOL_UPDATE                                          *
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       The messages are collected for the processing protocol.       *
*---------------------------------------------------------------------*

FORM protocol_update.

  CALL FUNCTION 'NAST_PROTOCOL_UPDATE'
    EXPORTING
      msg_arbgb = syst-msgid
      msg_nr    = syst-msgno
      msg_ty    = syst-msgty
      msg_v1    = syst-msgv1
      msg_v2    = syst-msgv2
      msg_v3    = syst-msgv3
      msg_v4    = syst-msgv4
    EXCEPTIONS
      OTHERS    = 1.

ENDFORM.                    "PROTOCOL_UPDATE
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  protocol_update_return
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
*      -->P_<LS_RETURN>  text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
FORM protocol_update_return USING p_return TYPE bapiret2.

  CALL FUNCTION 'NAST_PROTOCOL_UPDATE'
    EXPORTING
      msg_arbgb = p_return-id
      msg_nr    = p_return-number
      msg_ty    = p_return-type
      msg_v1    = p_return-message_v1
      msg_v2    = p_return-message_v2
      msg_v3    = p_return-message_v3
      msg_v4    = p_return-message_v4
    EXCEPTIONS
      OTHERS    = 1.

ENDFORM.                    " protocol_update_return

7. Final Architecture Principle

Standard-first approach

Low-coding strategy

Dynamic control via enhancement

Output-driven automation

Controlled customer-based exclusion

Lock-safe execution

8. Conclusion

This solution delivers a fully automated Order → Delivery → Goods Issue → Billing process while maintaining:

  • High adherence to SAP standards
  • Minimal custom development footprint
  • Flexible customer-level control
  • System stability and scalability

By combining standard configuration (VOV8, VV21), enhancement logic (MV45AFZZ), and output-based processing (REA0), an end-to-end automated and maintainable SD process was successfully implemented.

entry.title: Why SELECT returns different rows when querying a CDS View on MSEG versus MSEG directly
entry.content:
• Introduction
  • Custom CDS view ZCDS_E_MSEG
  • ABAP SELECT on MSEG
  • ABAP SELECT on ZCDS_E_MSEG
  • Why the Row Count Differs
  • Key Takeaways

 

Introduction

If you’ve recently worked with S/4HANA and created a custom CDS view on a standard table such as MSEG (*), you may have noticed something surprising:

Even if the CDS view is a simple SELECT FROM MSEG without any WHERE clause and there's no CDS Access Control implied, the number of rows returned by ABAP SELECT * FROM ZCDS_E_VIEW may not match the results of ABAP SELECT * FROM MSEG.

(*) You may find other tables like MSEG in the note 2206980 - Material Inventory Managment: change of data model in S/4HANA - SAP for Me.

 

Custom CDS view ZCDS_E_MSEG

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZCDS_V_MSEG'
define view ZCDS_E_MSEG as select from MSEG
{
  *
}

NB: a DDIC-based CDS view is used here to make the example simple, but as it's obsolete since ABAP 7.57 CDS view entities should be used instead.

 

ABAP SELECT on MSEG

SELECT * FROM MSEG
WHERE mblnr = '5000000000'
INTO TABLE (lt_mseg).
This example assumes there are some lines in MSEG for MBLNR = '5000000000'.
This ABAP SELECT may return more rows than the below ABAP SELECT on ZCDS_E_MSEG.
 

ABAP SELECT on ZCDS_E_MSEG

SELECT * FROM zcds_e_mseg
WHERE mblnr = '5000000000'
INTO TABLE (lt_cds_mseg).
Even though this query selects all rows from the CDS view ZCDS_E_MSEG (which is based on the physical MSEG table without any WHERE), the number of rows may be fewer than the ABAP SELECT on MSEG.
 

Why the Row Count Differs

  1. The table MSEG exists physically in the database but is no longer maintained

    • In S/4HANA, the real material document data lives in MATDOC.

    • The legacy table MSEG still exists in the database with the original ECC data, but is no more updated by S/4HANA transactions.

  2. ABAP SELECT on MSEG is redirected to the replacement object

    • When ABAP code does SELECT * FROM MSEG, SAP automatically redirects the query to the CDS replacement view NSDM_E_MSEG, which reads MATDOC.



      NB: NSDM_E_MSEG filters MATDOC using WHERE record_type = 'MDOC' but it's not implied in the problem discussed here.
      You can see in the SQL Trace (ST05) how ABAP SELECT ... FROM MSEG ... is automatically using the replacement object:

    • This redirection ensures that old programs continue to work correctly and return all material document lines, i.e. those lines migrated and updated in S/4HANA live.

  3. Custom CDS views on MSEG read the physical table directly

    • Any CDS view defined on MSEG does not use the replacement object, so it reads the actual content of the MSEG table, which are the data migrated from ECC, but are no more updated in S/4HANA.

    • If the table MSEG is empty in your system, it's because the Obsolete Data Handling tool has run (since S/4HANA 2023, it's the transaction code SODH, which has superseded ODH_DATA_PROCESSING). Reference: SAP S/4HANA Upgrade Guide, section Cleaning Up Obsolete Data After the Upgrade.

 

✅Key Takeaways

  • Do not rely on MSEG for new development; always use MATDOC or NSDM replacement views such as NSDM_E_MSEG.

  • NEVER use the old tables in your custom CDS views because they contain obsolete data.

  • After you have successfully tested and validated the upgrade results, you can clean up the obsolete data with the Obsolete Data Handling tool, to free space and to avoid the confusion and panic among developers.

entry.title: Introducing SAP Cloud ERP Private, Nonproduction Usage: A Flexible Path to SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction

Many SAP ERP Central Component (SAP ECC) cloud customers face a necessary upgrade because of upcoming end-of-support deadlines for SAP ERP 6.0 private cloud systems on Enhancement Packages 6-8 (mainstream maintenance ending in 2027 and extended maintenance in 2030).

SAP ECC (SAP ECC 6.0 with Enhancement Package 6 to 8 ) will move to Customer Specific Maintenance starting 1 January 2031. This also means that the SAP ERP, private edition offering will effectively have an end of availability on 31 December 2030. Because of this, many customers will face a time-sensitive transition period. During this time, they may need to run both their existing SAP ERP 6.0 system and the new SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud system in parallel—especially for non-production environments like testing, development, and QA during migration.

Having both environments available can accelerate transformation initiatives, allowing teams to leverage bundled cloud components for innovation, testing, process redesign, and early adoption of new capabilities while the legacy system continues to support ongoing operations.

At the same time, customers seeking a more cost-optimized transition path now have a new option that provides earlier access to a non-production environment without requiring a full parallel subscription. This gives customers greater flexibility to plan and execute their transformation while managing overall subscription costs.

To support this approach, we are introducing SAP Cloud ERP Private, non-production usage. This offering provides flexible, non-production usage of SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud during the transition period. Customers can perform migration and testing activities without requiring a full production subscription; however, appropriate non-production infrastructure capacity must still be provisioned / subscribed or repurposed from existing infrastructure.

How this helps, for example.

Explanation:

  • Without SAP Cloud ERP Private, nonproduction usage 
    In Year 2, the SAP ERP, private edition subscription is needed for the production systems, but as non-productive environments are gradually upgraded to the new S/4HANA Private Cloud version, they also need SAP Cloud ERP Private subscription. This overlap leads to simultaneous subscription costs of both SAP ERP, private edition and new SAP Cloud ERP Private systems. 
  • With SAP Cloud ERP Private, nonproduction usage 
    In Year 2, production continues under the old SAP ERP, private edition subscription, while the non-productive environments are covered by the new SAP Cloud ERP Private, nonproduction usage product. SAP Cloud ERP Private, nonproduction usage can be applied to an unlimited number of non-productive systems, allowing full coverage without triggering a full double subscription. In Year 3, all environments have migrated and can now be covered with the normal SAP Cloud ERP Private subscription.

Benefits of SAP Cloud ERP Private, nonproduction usage  

  • Removes simultaneous subscriptions during the transition phase 
  • Supports testing and validation without the need for exact timing of the switch to a new subscription 
  • Access to the SAP AI portfolio in the nonproduction environment, not available with SAP ECC [SAP ERP 6.0]
  • You only pay for limited support for the non-productive systems during the migration period
  • Covers an unlimited number of non-productive systems (non-productive systems must either be subscribed or repurposed from existing infra)
  • Gives customers the flexibility to plan and execute the migration properly
  • The transition period can be as long as needed, keeping the end of support date in mind.
  • SAP Cloud ERP Private, nonproduction usage is an addition to the SAP ERP, private edition subscription, extending the product support to S/4HANA Private Cloud in the transition phase. 
  • Helps organizations meet upgrade deadlines without budget overruns

entry.title: Pregúntame lo que quieras con SAP Champions – Serie mensual (Sesión 2)
entry.content:
Hola querida SAP Community,

Mi nombre es María Antonieta López, SAP Champion de México.

Actualmente, trabajo como Directora de Programas SAP, enfocada en programas globales de transformación SAP, implementaciones de SAP S/4HANA, arquitectura empresarial, marcos de gobernanza e innovación digital dentro del ecosistema SAP.

Como parte de los SAP Champions que lideran la iniciativa “Ask Me Anything” junto con Sebastiano Marchesini,

Me complace informarles que yo estaré liderando la edición de marzo.🚀

Así que sí…

Pueden preguntarme. LO QUE SEA.

 

Después de más de 20 años viviendo dentro de transformaciones SAP, probablemente he visto lo bueno, lo malo… y esos momentos de “¿cómo sobrevivió este sistema al go-live?” 😅

 

Durante las próximas semanas estaré respondiendo cualquier pregunta que tengan — sin filtros — solo SAP en vivo y real.

💡Podemos explorar temas como:

⚙️Implementaciones de SAP S/4HANA y programas globales de transformación – Ejecución de implementaciones end-to-end de SAP S/4HANA y programas de transformación a gran escala, permitiendo a las organizaciones modernizar sus procesos de negocio centrales, estandarizar operaciones globales y adoptar capacidades digitales de nueva generación.

🏗️ Arquitectura Empresarial – Procesos, artefactos y estrategias para la arquitectura empresarial de SAP basada en TOGAF.

🧼 Clean Core en SAP Cloud ERP (Private & Public) — procesos de gestión de proyectos para implementar o crear extensiones o interfaces en tu proyecto.

(Hablando de BUILD para configuradores y su uso con Joule, APIs preconstruidas, APIs nuevas, y por supuesto nuestra plataforma como servicio SAP Integration Suite, todo dentro de nuestra querida SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP)).

☁️ RISE o GROW con SAP + Gobernanza y metodología Clean Core

Desde SAP Activate hasta marcos como SAP Application Extension Methodology, ISA-M (Integration Solution Advisory Methodology), SAP Design Thinking, SAP Data Architecture Framework y, por qué no… incluso SAP Business AI Framework.

Puedes preguntar sobre qué artefactos usar, cómo ejecutar los procesos o por qué tengo que hacer esta tarea... Y te diré lo que pasará si te los saltas... Muahaha! …💀 así que mis historias de terror, como el invierno… están por llegar.

🏛️ Cómocrear un SAP Center of Excellence (CoE) o brindar servicios de SAP AM (Application Management) y realmente vivir felices para siempre con SAP usando Cloud ALM + Focused Build + Activate.

🌍 Globalización en SAP, ya sea de forma general o por país. ¡Larga vida a SAP Document & Reporting Compliance!

Y también a las extensiones DRC de nuestros partners SAP.

🧭 Roadmaps de soluciones y estrategia de transformación, etc

¿Y cualquier otra cosa? y ... ¡si no sé la respuesta! 👉 Prometo encontrar a alguien que sí la sepa… para ustedes.

 

Ya tenemos todo listo. 💬 Ahora es tu turno.

Deja tu pregunta en la sección de comentario👇 o puedes preguntar de forma anónima aquí 👉 AMA Champion Anonymously Questions o escaneando el QR Code 

 

📅Esta edición del AMA estará abierta para preguntas hasta el 27 de marzo de 2026.

Después de esa fecha, las preguntas seleccionadas serán respondidas en un video grabado que se publicará en el canal de YouTube de la SAP Community  👉 @SAPCommunity

También se publicará un blog resumen con las preguntas y respuestas para la comunidad.

 

Cuéntame…

🤓¿Qué tema de SAP te gustaría que exploremos juntos?

entry.title: Clean Core Is a Leadership Discipline, Not a Technical Rule
entry.content:
I recently took an SAP course about Clean Core, and it left me thinking about how people interpret the concept in practice. Most explanations focus on ways to keep the system as simple as possible, avoiding changing the original software too much. Personally, what we keep seeing in real SAP transformations that our clients bring to our table is that Clean Core doesn’t necessarily represent an issue because of architecture, but rather because of people. 

The tension around Clean Core becomes apparent the moment a decision maker asks to adjust something “just a little”, explains that their processes work a bit differently, or insists that there’s only one way things have always been done. Of course, there are several ways to solve the request, but my question is: Should the change be implemented at all in the first place? And here is where our concern starts, because this is not a technical decision, but a leadership one.

Clean Core Is Not About Restricting the Business

Clean Core is sometimes interpreted as saying “no” to the business, as if it were about restricting flexibility or forcing standardization just because. In reality, it has more to do with protecting the future of the system. With the migration to cloud ERP and faster upgrade cycles, customization starts to play a different role. Things that used to be manageable in traditional on-premise systems can become way more complicated in a cloud environment. Besides the problems of costs or upgrades, customizations introduce a deeper issue: it becomes less clear who is responsible for what.

This became clear after assessing many of our clients’ landscapes, where heavily customized processes often resulted in unclear ownership. Who owns the process: the business or IT? Who decides when and what needs to change? Over time, these customizations tend to end up in a strange situation where no one really owns, questions or removes them. 

Clean Core introduces a different kind of conversation before all those issues come up. It pushes organizations to ask whether a customization is necessary at all: Does it really create value or is it just the protection of a legacy habit? Once again, we are talking about leadership decisions that have almost nothing to do with architecture. 

What Pressure to Customize Often Reveals

Across multiple client transformations, what the strongest pressure to customize reveals are organizational dynamics. Sometimes, there are governance gaps, where decisions are made without a clear framework or roadmap. Other times, as mentioned earlier, ownership is unclear and different teams defend their own variations of a process. In other cases, deadlines and internal pressure push teams towards quick fixes as temporary solutions instead of redesigning processes. Finally, sometimes customization simply reflects resistance to change. 

These dynamics don’t disappear just because we introduce better extensibility tools. They tend to improve only when leadership alignment improves, and Clean Core works when teams are clear about when it actually makes sense to move away from standard. The key is being intentional. In the projects we’ve worked on, the teams that manage this better are the ones that pause and ask themselves simple questions like if a customization will create problems later on or if it would make upgrades harder. 

Our Experience

Recently, we worked on a S/4 project for a company that had started using drones to collect field data for forecasting. The goal was to move beyond manual reports and gain more real-time visibility from physical assets. Of course, Clean Core was part of the strategy from the beginning, as the goal was to keep S/4 standard and integrate new capabilities in a sustainable way.

Once the workshops started, the situation became more complicated. The forecasting team suggested adding custom fields in inventory and maintenance documents so the drone data could be stored directly in the S/4 tables. It wasn’t an unreasonable idea, we understood that they just wanted the data where everything else already lived.

With that idea on the table, the conversation started moving forward. If the drone data was already in S/4, why not use it directly in the planning logic? There was even the suggestion of modifying the material planning process so the drone data could automatically adjust demand forecasts. What they were asking for was technically possible, and thinking back on, it would’ve probably been faster to just build it directly in the core. This was exactly the moment where all I’ve stated about Clean Core became a reality for us, we had no other choice but to ask ourselves if this logic should really live inside the S/4 core.

In the end, we decided to keep the drone data outside of S/4 and bring the results back through standard interfaces. Yes, it took a bit more coordination, but the core system stayed clean. 

What that project made clear to us is how these situations usually start. At the beginning everyone agrees with the idea of keeping the system standard. But once the workshops begin, small requests start appearing. None of them sound dramatic and, again, they are technically possible and even make sense. But that’s usually how heavy customization slowly creeps back into the system.

Clean Core as Cultural Discipline

In the end, Clean Core says a lot about how an organization makes decisions. In our experience, it depends less on tools and more on leadership, ownership, and how teams make decisions during a transformation. Staying “clean” is not about having a perfect system, but about pausing before customizing and asking whether the change is truly necessary. 

When Clean Core breaks down, it is rarely because the technology couldn’t support the extension. More often it happens when ownership is unclear or short-term pressure overrides long-term thinking. In that sense, Clean Core ends up revealing how disciplined an organization really is when making technology decisions.

entry.title: From Manual Counting to Real-Time Visibility in SAP
entry.content:
Some time ago, we worked with a company that provides pipe manufacturing and related services for the world’s energy industry and other industrial applications.  Although the company operates in many countries and has more than 20,000 employees, the tracking of tubular components for low-carbon energy applications was still a manual task before we started working together. This meant that operators had to count materials directly in the field and then register that information later.

Tracking these materials was particularly challenging given the scale of the operation and the environments in which it took place. Tubular components were distributed across multiple plants and locations, many of them with limited connectivity. This made it difficult to capture and update information in real time, increasing the chances of delays and inconsistencies. Over time, these conditions led to inefficiencies, operational risks, and a negative impact on productivity.

Our original goal was to make the process faster and reduce errors. The most straightforward approach would have been to digitize what already existed by replacing the manual input with a mobile application. Although this would have fixed how data was captured, it would not have addressed another important issue: whether that data could actually be trusted and used in real time.

To address the situation in a general way, and not just part of it, we implemented a solution that included not only a mobile application but that was also based on image recognition. Using the application, operators were able to capture images of the materials, and the system would automatically identify and count the tubular components. This allowed the process to move from manual counting to a more automated and reliable approach.

The mobile application was not limited to image-based counting. It supported daily operations in the field, which allowed operators to execute instructions, manage materials, perform inspections, and update statuses directly from the device. Tasks such as inspections adjustments or managing dispatching processes were all carried out within that same application, reducing the need for manual steps and enabling more consistent work. Not to mention that such an application worked both online and offline and was connected to RFID readers and wireless printers. This made things even faster and reduced errors in material handling.

Deciding how information was going to move from the field into the system was another key aspect. Instead of being manually recorded and later uploaded, the data captured through the application was directly synchronized, making it available as part of the operational flow.

All this information was synchronized with SAP ecosystem. Instead of being delayed or manually transferred, the data captured in the field was updated and made available as part of ongoing processes. This meant that material movements, status changes, and tracking information could be reflected more consistently across the system, without depending on later adjustments or corrections. As a result, teams had access to more reliable and up-to-date information, making it easier to manage operations on a daily basis.

By replacing manual steps with a more automated approach, the process became more consistent and safer for the people involved. This was reflected in a 35% increase in productivity and a 47% drop in work-related incidents, but more importantly, in a process that was easier and more reliable to handle on a daily basis.

entry.title: What’s New in Product Change Management and why should I care?
entry.content:
Welcome to this blog post where I will share the latest updates on Product Change Management in SAP Cloud ERP Private including an exciting sneak preview of our upcoming Change Management Agent.  Please note that Change Management Agent is a BETA version, any version that will be generally available in the future may work differently!

Nowadays, product change management requires speed, control, and compliance to operate in lockstep. Organizations need a clear, repeatable process to capture issues, assess impact, and implement changes across engineering, manufacturing, and quality—without adding risk or rework. That foundation depends on a centralized system of record, defined roles and workflows, transparent approvals, and strong auditability.

No time to read? For more details about the latest updates in change management, including FAQs, sign up and access the recording of our latest Meet the Expert session.

A modern change process starts with clear problem definition, followed by structured evaluation and disciplined execution. Each stage relies on consistent data capture, governance, and cross-functional visibility. The core artifact is a change record that links people, processes, and products. It assigns accountable teams, enforces workflow steps, captures digital signatures, and consolidates all affected objects—materials, BOMs, specs, recipes, and related documents. Mass assignment lets users add multiple materials and document records in a single action, cutting administrative work. Regulatory modes tighten controls where needed, blocking out-of-process edits and keeping every change on a defined path. This creates a compliant framework that scales across product lines and business units.

With the past releases, we added a number of new features to help your companies reach their goals: 

Enhancements to Change Objects:

 

  • Document info record, production routing, task lists and product variant structure as  supported objects for regulatory mode
  • Smart links enhancements
  • Custom objects, mass assignment of objects

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

 

Enhancements to Process Routes:

  • Add creator/responsible of CR as workflow recipient
  • Send email for process route activities (Approve, Reject, etc.)
  • Access attachments of workflow items in CR
  • CR embedded in My Inbox
  • Dynamic team determination

 

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

Usability is pivotal for adoption and data quality. Copy functions allow users to replicate a change record with only the needed sections—descriptions, items, documents—speeding creation without carrying noise forward. Rich text editing and resizable description panes promote complete, in-line documentation, improving downstream search and review. Default relevance types for change items remove repetitive selections while preserving exceptions. Workflow updates route tasks to creators or accountable owners, embed tasks in the inbox with full context, trigger email notifications, and require digital signatures at the task level. Traceability strengthens without slowing execution. These pragmatic improvements reduce cycle time and make governance feel embedded, not imposed.

Enhancements to Usability and Collaboration:

  • Copy change records
  • Enlarged detailed description with rich text editor
  • Default item relevance for change items

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

 

Enhancements to Digital Signature:

  • Digital signature as part of workflow

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

And what about AI? How can it help me perform my daily work more efficiently?

AI extends this rigor by removing friction from high-frequency tasks and surfacing insight at the moment of decision. Traditional search and review across change records, documents, routings, and BOMs is slow and fragmented, especially across multiple record types and regions, which drives duplication, missed context, and delays. Conversational assistance for ad hoc queries and summarization enables engineers and reviewers to locate, understand, and act on change data in minutes rather than hours.

By targeting search, filtering, traceability, and quick summaries first, teams gain immediate productivity without reengineering the process landscape. The result is faster cycles with proven compliance, supported by a system that maintains control while accelerating decision-making.

 

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

 

Can we go a few steps further?

Already today, targeted AI amplifies productivity gains by removing bottlenecks. Our Change Record Management Agent supports traceability, audit compliance, and greater efficiency in managing product and design changes through where-used analysis, change impact summarization, similarity search and agent-guided creation of new change records.

  • Improves change coordinator’s productivity
  • Reduces engineering change cost
  • Reduces cost of new product introduction to market.

 

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

 

On the way: AI-Assisted Similarity Check, Change Record Creation, and Summarization

We won’t stop there: Soon, our conversational assistant will help you cut rework and improve decisions. Run similarity checks across all change items, get intelligent proposals for change creation, and workflows. Done well and with the help of AI, change management becomes a strategic capability—compliant by design, collaborative by default, and continuously optimized by data—so teams find the right records fast, grasp context in seconds, and initiate well‑formed changes with less effort.

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

 

For more details about the latest updates in change management, including FAQs, sign up and access the recording of our latest Meet the Expert session.

More Information / Additional Links 

Also, check out our What’s New document for regular updates!

Don’t hesitate to leave a comment and refer to our community page to ask your questions and engage with the experts. Follow the PSCC_Enablement tag to stay up to date with our latest blog posts. 

Follow me on LinkedIn and stay up-to-date to our latest enablement assets: linkedin.com/in/annette-garcia-lorenzo

entry.title: Solution Order Management in SAP Cloud ERP 2602
entry.content:
The SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 update continues our commitment to delivering intelligent, flexible, and user-centric enhancements. This update introduces powerful new capabilities specifically designed to streamline processes for Solution Order Specialists. New capabilities have been introduced covering the full scope of solution selling with SAP Cloud ERP, and in this blog, we’ll look at them in more detail.

Customer Hierarchy and Product Hierarchy related Pricing in Solution Orders

 

You can now leverage product hierarchies directly within the Manage Solution Orders app. Product hierarchies are a cornerstone among the generic features for organizing products, or materials, across multiple levels. This structured approach is particularly valuable for complex pricing scenarios. When your price calculations are configured to depend on product hierarchy data, you can now use these hierarchies in your solution orders for precise and automated price determination, simplifying a critical part of the sales process.

Similar to the enhancement for products, you can now utilize customer hierarchies in the Manage Solution Orders app. Organizing customers into multi-level hierarchies is often essential for managing pricing agreements and sales strategies. With this feature, you can now use customer hierarchies within solution orders for price determination, provided your pricing procedures are set up to recognize this data. This enables more accurate, automated pricing tailored to your customer structures.

For an in-depth look at customer and product hierarchies in solution order management, take a look at this blog

Sales Item Enhancements in 2602

Enhanced Flexibility with New Item Categories and Process Variants

As a solution order specialist, compiling sales orders that meet specific business needs and align with customer agreements is essential. This requires a high degree of flexibility, With 2602, we are extending solution orders to cover sales processes that were previously unsupported for configurable products and sales kits. The Manage Solution Orders app is made more versatile with the introduction of new item categories and process variants, opening the door for selling high-value, make-to-order items.

 



From 2602, we support milestone billing in solution orders. Milestone billing is handled with a billing plan that splits total sales-order value across predefined billing dates, instead of billing everything at once -typically used for long duration deliveries or projects. Introducing this capability also applies milestone-based billing to established sales kit scenarios such as ERLA. LUMF and CPFH. Furthermore, it provides solution order specialists with the option to manage down payments as part of solution selling.

 

Alongside milestone-based billing, we introduce the ability to run advanced down payment (ADP) processing for sales orders that are created as follow-ups to solution orders. This enhancement integrates your solution order process more tightly with your financial operations, securing revenue upfront for large or custom orders. Before utilizing this feature, a configuration expert must ensure the condition types DDPR and DDPS have been added to the pricing procedure used for your solution orders.

New item categories introduced to the manage solution orders app support third-party sales with variant configuration. We have expanded solution order capabilities for configurable products by adding support for third-party shipment scenarios. This should help business users handle a broader range of sales scenarios within a single framework and better orchestrate complex fulfillment models involving external suppliers. This enhancement makes it possible to manage third-party sales in make-to-order processes, both with and without charge, giving companies more flexibility in how they structure customer-specific solution offerings.

 

Overall, these changes strengthen solution selling by enabling more adaptable end-to-end sales processes. They also improve business-user productivity by making it easier to manage customer-specific requirements through a more flexible and comprehensive solution order process.

This update is critical for businesses that manage high-value sales items, as it helps optimize cash flow through milestone-based invoicing. It also delivers a more flexible, customer-centric approach to sales order processing by better accommodating complex product configurations in third-party scenarios.

See the documentation for a full overview of new item categories

 

Set Billing Blocks for Release and Completed Sales Items (2602.1)

A helpful enhancement in the Manage Solution Orders app now lets users set or reset billing blocks even after sales items have reached Released or Completed status. That means billing issues can be corrected later in the process without reopening documents, helping teams work more efficiently and keep billing moving. Billing blocks are maintained by specifying the relevant billing block reason. Previously, this was only possible while sales items were still Open or In Process. There are a couple of important conditions to keep in mind: this feature is available for sales items released for follow-up sales orders of type OR, while items released for sales orders of type SOR have always allowed billing block updates. The same capability is also available through the business solution order APIs, including API_BUS_SOLUTION_ORDER_SRV and BusinessSolutionOrder.

Cumulated net value field for solution order items in the solution order output form

 

Solution bundles let you sell a combined offering in a solution order using one bundle item with predefined subitems such as products, services, projects, contracts, or subscriptions. SAP treats the bundle item as a configurable product and, after release, generates the relevant follow-up documents for the underlying subitems. When selling preconfigured bundles, customers wish to indicate the bundle price in the order confirmation they issue to customers to provide more transparency. As a result, with 2602, we make a cumulated net value field available in the output interface and forms. This shows the aggregated price of the purchased bundle without a breakdown of the individual items in the bundle.

 

Transfer of Transactional Address to Sales Orders

To provide greater flexibility in managing address information for complex business scenarios, business users can now define specific transactional addresses for business partners (category as organization) at the solution order level. This address information is then seamlessly transferred to any subsequent sales orders created from that solution order.

Note: While a change of address on subsequent documents is permitted and will be reflected throughout the document chain, this action is recommended only for making minor typographical corrections to maintain data consistency.


Filter by Solution Order When Scheduling Billing Document Creation (2508.1)

 

A feature which was released in the first micro-delivery of the 2508 release which we want to draw your attention to is the support of scheduled billing creation with solution orders. Previously, filtering by solution order was only available in the Create Billing Documents app. Now, the same capability is available in Schedule Billing Creation, allowing users either to select billing-relevant documents for specific solution orders or to include all solution-order-related billing due list items in a recurring job. This supports both specialized solution-selling scenarios, where only selected solution orders should be billed, and broader operational setups where solution-order processing is used more widely alongside other sales scenarios.

To support this, we have introduced a new Solution Order field under SD Document Data in the app. As part of the UI update, the SD Document field was also moved under SD Document Data, and Documents to Be Selected was renamed to SD Documents to Be Selected. Overall, this enhancement helps organizations automate invoicing for solution-order-based business more efficiently, reduce manual effort, and process larger billing volumes faster.

 

Filtered Date Rules for Service Contract Billing in Solution Orders

We have increased clarity and convenience in billing plan management. You can now use filtered date rules, configured by key users, for the billing plan of service contract items in solution orders. This applies to both settlement period rules and billing date rules. Previously, the Manage Solution Orders app displayed all available rules, which required users to rely on descriptive texts to find the correct one. This new, filtered approach ensures that only relevant rules are presented, significantly reducing errors and improving user efficiency.

Subscription Items Enhancements in Solution Order Management (2508.3)

Selling subscriptions within solution orders has been significantly enhanced in the 2508 microdelivery releases, and further with 2602. Below, you’ll find an overview of key new capabilities introduced to make subscription selling more reliable, flexible, and user-friendly.


Subscription Validation in Solution Orders (2508.3)

One of the most valuable updates is support for the subscription validation extension point from SAP Subscription Billing. This helps business users prevent the creation of incorrect or incomplete follow-up subscriptions by validating subscription data during simulation and release. As a result, errors can be caught earlier in the process, improving productivity and reducing the need for later corrections.

 


Auto-Locking for subscription item prices (2508.3)

A major usability improvement is the new Auto-Lock pricing capability. With this feature, prices can be locked automatically once a subscription is created, protecting the agreed subscription price from later changes in the underlying product list price. In the Solution Order UI, each price element now shows a Customization Status, indicating whether it is customizable or non-customizable, along with a new Auto-Lock column that shows whether the price is currently locked. This gives users much better transparency into pricing behavior and helps improve customer satisfaction by ensuring stable subscription pricing over time.

The enhancement supports three pricing scenarios: customizable only, auto-locked only, and both customizable and auto-locked. When both settings are enabled, the price can be customized once, after which the auto-lock is removed. These capabilities are powered by new fields in the subscription API, which provide the necessary status information and ensure a more consistent and intuitive user experience across the process.

The release also increases flexibility in commercial subscription models by adding support for both credit and charge rate elements. This means businesses can now model one-time credits as well as one-time charges directly in Solution Order, enabling more creative and customer-friendly offerings such as onboarding credits, promotional incentives, or service compensation.

Alternative currencies for subscription items

Subscriptions can now be created in an alternate market currency, making it easier to satisfy customer-specific regional or contractual requirements when the subscription currency differs from the main Solution Order transaction currency. Together, these enhancements make subscription handling more robust, transparent, and adaptable for real-world solution selling.

 

Integration

Simulation API for Solution Orders for external Quoting applications

Building on the 2508 release, we have made further enhancements to the Solution Order API with OData V4 simulation capabilities that allow businesses to simulate solution orders without creating actual transactional documents. This gives external quoting and sales applications access to SAP Cloud ERP logic in real time, making it easier to generate accurate quotes based on partner determination, pricing, and billing plan simulation.

The API returns detailed simulation results at both header and item level, including net value, partners, price elements, and contract billing plans for solution orders and their items. It supports a broad range of item types, such as sales items, service contract items, and service order items, while also covering scenarios like sales kits, service bundles, and preconfigured solution bundles.

These capabilities improve integration flexibility and support more seamless orchestration across external sales tools and SAP Cloud ERP. With built-in extensibility and secure OAuth 2.0-based integration, the Solution Order API provides a strong foundation for connected sales processes, while future roadmap plans include support for configurable products, parameterized solution bundles, and subscription products.

 

 


Extensibility Enhancements

Enable Subscription billing cloud BAdI for developer extensibility

Extensibility is a critical success factor in customer implementation projects, especially when standard capabilities in solution business and service management need to be enhanced to meet specific business requirements. Recent innovations strengthen both developer and side-by-side extensibility. On the developer extensibility side, READ access through Entity Manipulation Language (EML) is now available for BAdI CL_CRMS4_SERV_CHCK_BEFORE_SAVE, enabling the use of RAP façades to access the transient data buffer introduced in release 2602. In addition, BAdI CRMS4_SOLO_ORC_SB_SUBSC_MODIFY has now been officially released, giving developers more flexibility to adapt subscription-related processes. For side-by-side extensibility, “derived” events based on standard events make it possible to enrich event payloads and define context attributes for more precise filtering. This capability is already available for Service Contract in 2602, with Solution Order support planned from 2608 onward.



 

 Find all our assets

Do you want to learn more about the main innovations in different lines of business in the SAP Cloud ERP 2602 release? The release 2602 showcases more 130 innovation highlights across the portfolio. Check out our release document for all the details!

 

Enablement

We at Cloud ERP and SCM Product Success offer a service as versatile as our product itself. Click on the enablement wheel below and check out the numerous offerings our team has created for you: 

 

 

 

 ️ Watch the replays of our SAP Cloud ERP 2602 Early Release Series!

From January 12-16, we hosted a series of 22 expert-led live sessions to highlight the exciting innovations shipped with the SAP Cloud ERP 2602 release. Missed the live sessions? We've got you covered! Take advantage of the entire series or pick and choose the sessions most relevant to you. You will find the recording and presentation for Asset Management as well as 21 other topics of interest. Don't miss this chance and watch them on demand – anywhere, anytime.

entry.title: How the condition value (XKOMV-KWERT) is calculated in subtotal pricing conditions
entry.content:
It is not always clear for some end users how the subtotal condition value is calculated.
In the following blog post, I will explain it in details.

Different from what is observed for pricing conditions, subtotal value is calculated in a different way.

For example, in the below scenario, we observe that subtotal 'Net value + Tax' has condition value calculated as '10.559,05', but most of the users would expect its calculation as '58,99 * 179 = 10.559,21', considering the quantity as '179':





According to note 0001007110, mainly the following part, subtotal value is calculated in the following way:

...
This consulting note describes how the condition value (the XKOMV-KWERT field) of a subtotal (lines of the pricing procedure without a condition type) is determined in the pricing result.
...

2. One or more reference steps are maintained:

The condition value of the subtotal is determined in the same way as the condition basis is determined by 'value-related' conditions.
See Note 0000834174, section 2.
...


Then, from note 0000834174 - section 2:

...

2. One or more reference steps are maintained:

Only step numbers that are smaller than the current reference step can be chosen as reference steps. This is because the condition values of following step numbers have not yet been determined due to the successive processing of the pricing procedure (see above).

If only the from-reference step is maintained, the system internally fills the to-reference step with the from-reference step. In this case therefore only this one reference step is relevant.
If both the from-reference step and the to-reference step (greater or equal to the from-reference step) are maintained, all step numbers are relevant, that are greater than or equal to the from-reference step and smaller than or equal to the to-reference step.
To determine the condition basis, the system now adds the condition values of all relevant conditions that are not inactive with an 'AKLMX' indicator (see Note 836243).
It should be noted that in this case pricing conditions with the inactivity indicator 'Y' are taken into account during the determination of the condition basis, as long as their step number is in the relevant area.
In addition, the system also takes subtotals into account, as long as their step number is in the relevant area.
...

Therefore, when checking the related pricing procedure, we see that subtotal 'Net value + Tax' has 'from/to' steps referring interval of '10 to 80', which refers to pricing conditions 'PR00' and 'MWST', which are the existent ones in the example above.



Hence, subtotal condition value (KWERT) for 'Net value + Tax' will be calculated as 'PR00 + MWST', resulting in '9.868,27 + 690,78 = 10.599,05'.

Technically speaking, this takes place in the logic below:

Include LV61AA55
Form XKOMV_BEWERTEN 
...
      ELSE.
        IF xkomv-kschl EQ space.
          PERFORM xkomv_kwert_ermitteln.
        ENDIF.
      ENDIF.
...


Now, to calculate the condition amount (KBETR) for subtotals, system performs a backward calculation, as explained in note 0000791944, i.e.:

...
2. In general, the condition rate of a subtotal is determined by dividing the condition value of the subtotal (see Note 1007110) by the condition basis of the pricing condition (YKMENG) ("backward calculation").
...


Then, for subtotal 'Net value + Tax', condition rate will be calculated as '10.599,05 / 179 = 58,99'.


Technically speaking:

Include LV61AA43
Form XKOMV_KWERT_ERMITTELN
...
* price per unit if sum line
  IF xkomv-kschl = space.
    PERFORM xkomv_kbetr_from_kwert USING xkomv-kwert.
  ENDIF.
...


If a different behavior is required for calculating the subtotal condition rate or value, a custom formula should be developed.

I hope this clarifies this recurrent topic.

entry.title: New Complaint Against Supplier Process in SAP Quality Management
entry.content:
New Complaint Against Supplier Process in SAP Quality Management

Supplier-related quality issues are a common topic for many SAP Quality Management practitioners. Defective deliveries, missing documentation, or repeated nonconformances often require structured follow-up—yet in practice, information is frequently spread across emails, documents, or loosely connected transactions.

With SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition 2602, Quality Management introduces a Complaint Against Supplier process. This new capability is integrated into Nonconformance Management and provides a central object to document, track, and process supplier-related quality complaints.

This post gives an overview of how the process works and what is currently delivered.

What Is the Complaint Against Supplier?

The Complaint Against Supplier is a dedicated Quality Management object used to document issues caused by suppliers and to manage related follow-up activities.

It supports:

  • Central documentation of supplier complaints
  • Linking supplier complaints to existing defects
  • Triggering follow-up actions directly from the complaint
  • Structured and traceable communication with suppliers

The goal is not to replace existing QM processes, but to complement them with a clearer supplier-focused view.

Creating and Managing a Supplier Complaint

A complaint against a supplier can be created by entering key information such as:

  • A short and detailed description of the issue
  • Planned start/end dates and reference numbers
  • Supplier and other involved roles
  • Defects to be assigned to the complaint

Additional documents (for example inspection results or images) can be attached to provide supporting evidence.

Once created, the complaint serves as the central point of reference throughout its lifecycle.

Linking Defects and Follow-Up Activities

Existing defects (all defects with a material/plant reference) can be assigned to a supplier complaint. This helps avoid duplicate data entry and ensures that the defect context is preserved.

From the complaint, follow-up activities such as creating a returns purchase order can be triggered, allowing the operational handling of the issue to remain connected to the QM documentation.

Communicating with Suppliers

The process is integrated with Output Management, enabling the generation of a standardized Complaint Report.

The report can be sent to the supplier:

  • As a printout
  • By email
  • Or via an external output management system

This provides a consistent and traceable way to share complaint information with suppliers.

Change Tracking and Transparency

All relevant changes made to a supplier complaint are logged. This supports:

  • Transparency during complaint processing
  • Traceability for audits or reviews
  • Better understanding of how issues were resolved

Availability

The Complaint Against Supplier process is available with SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition 2602 as part of Quality Management – Nonconformance Management.

Outlook

Further enhancements are planned for upcoming releases, including:

  • Creating complaints directly from the Manage Defect worklist
  • Posting defective material to scrap from within a supplier complaint
  • Supporting key user extensibility with custom fields

Details and timelines may change and should be followed through official release information.

Demo

Here is a short system demo of how this feature works.

 

Conclusion

The new Complaint Against Supplier process adds a structured way to handle supplier-related quality issues in SAP Quality Management. By linking complaints, defects, follow-up actions, and supplier communication, it supports greater transparency and consistency in nonconformance handling.

If you are already working with supplier quality processes, this enhancement may be worth exploring in your system.

💬Questions or experience to share?
Feel free to leave a comment below or share how you currently manage supplier complaints in QM.

entry.title: Alphanumeric CNPJ: What You Need to Know
entry.content:
Brazil’s tax reform introduces several structural changes across the fiscal landscape, and one of the most impactful is the adoption of the alphanumeric CNPJ. As this new identifier takes shape, SAP systems must be ready.

This blog provides an overview of the change, explains why it is being introduced, outlines the SAP areas affected, and highlights the delivery timelines and key references you should be aware of to prepare for compliance.

What Is Changing?

The Brazilian CNPJ (National Register of Legal Entities) has always been a 14-digit numeric identifier. Starting in July 2026, it will expand to include both letters and numbers, becoming an alphanumeric identifier. This change applies only to newly issued CNPJs, existing ones remain unchanged.

This change aims to:

  • Increase the available combination space for taxpayer identification
  • Improve the classification of entities, branches, and establishments
  • Provide more flexibility and scalability for the new tax model

SAP's Preparation and Timeline

SAP is actively updating its solutions to accommodate the new format. The central reference is SAP Note 3714871 – Tax Reform: Alphanumeric CNPJ Leading SAP Note, which consolidates guidance, impacted objects, delivery plans, and related documentation.

This is the primary source you should follow.

Delivery plans can be accessed through the following links:

  • Tax Reform - Alphanumeric CNPJ - Normative Instruction No. 2229, of October 15,
  • Tax Reform - Joint Technical Note 2025.001 - Alphanumeric CNPJ

Key Areas Affected

SAP has identified several modules and processes affected by the new CNPJ format. Key areas include:

  • Business Partner: Fields now support alphanumeric input with updated validation rules and error messages
    • Adjustment delivered in SAP Note 3583627
  • Logistics and Nota Fiscal Database: Structural updates to tables, NF-e repository, integration frameworks and tax transactions
  • Document and Reporting Compliance (NF-e): Updates to electronic nota fiscal, digital signature, and schema validations
  • Human Capital Management (HCM) Brazil (Payroll): Adaptation of eSocial solution (layouts, event tables, and extraction process), employer records, employee master data and HCM reports
  • Finance and Banking: Changes to CNAB files, PIX, TED, and Boleto payment methods, updates in routines for generating payment files

SAP System Changes in Customizing (IMG)

Adjustments already delivered within the SAP Customizing environment include:

Global Company Parameters

SPRO → Financial Accounting → Financial Accounting Global Settings → Global Parameters for Company Code → Maintain Additional Parameters

Business Place Definition

SPRO → Cross-Application Components → General Application Functions → Nota fiscal  →  CNPJ Business Places  →  Define Business Places for Brazil

These areas now fully support alphanumeric CNPJ values.

ABAP Objects Impacted

SAP Note 3714871 provides a detailed list of ABAP objects expected to be modified. These objects are being converted from numeric to character-based (CHAR) fields to support the new identifier.

SAP recommends that you review custom developments (Z objects) and integrations that reference numeric CNPJ fields, and other objects that contain the CNPJ (access key), as these may need adjustments.

This includes:

  • Tables
  • Data elements
  • Domain definitions
  • Interfaces
  • Business Add-In (BAdI) implementations
  • Enhancements
  • Custom reports

Planned Delivery Timeline

Important: The expected delivery dates are subject to change without prior notice and represent only an estimate provided by SAP, not a contractual commitment for delivery.

HCM Brazil (Payroll)

  • Phase 1: March 13, 2026
  • Phase 2: March 31, 2026

Logistics and Nota Fiscal Database Adjustments

  • April 17, 2026 (Prerequisite for all other Logistics, Document and Reporting Compliance, and Financial updates)

Document and Reporting Compliance (NF-e) – Public Cloud and Private Cloud/On-Premise

  • April 17, 2026

Business Partner and Statutory Reports

  • April 17, 2026

Banking Files – CNAB / Payment Methods

  • CNAB Private Cloud/On-Premise: April 16, 2026FICA Private Cloud/On-Premise (PIX, TED, Boleto included): April 16, 2026

Key References

SAP Note

  • 3714871 – Tax Reform: Alphanumeric CNPJ Leading SAP Note

Regulatory Change Manager Links

  • HCM Impacts – Phase 1 and Phase 2
  • Nota Fiscal Database Adjustments
  • Business Partner and Statutory Reports
  • CNAB Files
  • CNAB Files FICA

Note: The links provided are specific to the Product Impact of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. For other products, such as SAP S/4HANA , SAP ERP Central Component, and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, refer to the regulatory change manager links related to each product.

What Should You Do Now?

Start by reviewing SAP Note 3714871 and evaluating any custom developments that reference CNPJ fields, and other objects that contain the CNPJ (access key). Early preparation will help minimize disruption and ensure your organization stays compliant as Brazil's tax infrastructure evolves.

In summary, your action plan should be:

  1. Review SAP Note 3714871 in detail
  2. Evaluate all custom developments that reference CNPJ fields, and other objects that contain the CNPJ (access key)
  3. Track delivery updates via the regulatory change manager
  4. Plan for early testing in sandbox environment
  5. Update internal processes and integrations (banks, partners, NF-e service providers, tax engines, etc.)

Early preparation will help you avoid disruption and maintain compliance as Brazil's tax landscape evolves. The clock is ticking, but with the right approach, you can navigate this transition smoothly.

Update 27 April, 2026

Communication Regarding Planned Delivery

As part of a recent technical validation, an incompatibility was identified that requires a revision of the originally planned approach. To ensure system consistency and protect your landscape, the corresponding change has been temporarily reverted. At this time, no action is required from you. 

A new solution, based on an improved technical approach, is currently under development and will be made available shortly. We appreciate your understanding and reaffirm our commitment to delivering stable, high-quality solutions.

Update 06 May, 2026

SAP Notes have been released delivering a new solution based on an improved technical approach. You should review and follow the instructions provided in SAP Note 3714871 – Tax Reform: Alphanumeric CNPJ Leading SAP Note.

For more information or if you have any questions about the Brazilian tax reform,  visit the dedicated Tax Reform Work Zone — your hub for updates, resources, and deeper insights.

Did you enjoy this post? Give it a Like and share it with your colleagues! Feel free to leave your feedback or comments in the space below. And remember to follow the SAP S/4HANA Logistics for Brazil tag in the SAP Community to stay tuned for the latest news. 

 
Thanks for reading — until next time!
Luize

entry.title: I reverse‑engineered MM invoice accounting (and here's your new automatic analysis tool!)
entry.content:
I’ve spent the last few years in SAP Support staring at MM invoices that “don’t make sense.”
If you recognize subjects like:

  • “GRIR can not be cleared”

  • “Posting and reversal amount does not match”
  • “Exchange rate difference between GR and IR settling to material account”
  • “Unexpected UMB revaluation posting in MIRO”

…then you already know the kind of stories I’m talking about.

In theory, GR/IR should be simple: goods go in, invoice comes later, the clearing account should end up at zero and reversals should “just mirror” the original. In practice, you add price changes, condition updates, partial goods receipts, provisional/final invoicing, several currencies, and postings across periods – and suddenly the GR/IR line or the reversal document looks like it came from another universe.

For a long time, all we could do would be to share KBAs explaining the standard design and the complex calculations that actually occur in the back-end. This is not efficient, and increases both engineer effort and customer effort.

So I decided to do something slightly extreme: reverse‑engineer the MM invoice accounting logic and encode it into a report that tells the story for you.

The old way

If you’ve ever tried to really understand a tricky MIRO or MR8M document, you’ve probably met some of these notes:

  • 1674290 – Invoice Posting Logic – SAP ERP & SAP S/4HANA

  • 1609927 – Credit Memo Posting Logic – SAP ERP & SAP S/4HANA
  • 1789924 – Subsequent Debit/Credit Posting Logic – SAP ERP & SAP S/4HANA
  • 3382819 – MR11/MR11SHOW: Calculation logic for GR/IR clearing – SAP ERP & SAP S/4HANA
  • 3039200 – MIRO: KDM and PRD differences – SAP ERP & SAP S/4HANA

Together, they explain the core clearing logic very well: which quantities and values are used, how WRX is derived, what happens when subsequent debits/credits are handled, what's different in MR11, and how KDM/PRD differences are supposed to look.

The problem is not the documentation; the problem is how much work it takes to apply it:

  • You need to dig out all GR and IR documents for the PO item.

  • You need to consider the right currency context.
  • You need to apply the formulas on top of the customer’s specific history.
  • And you need to do it again for the next incident.

This is exactly where the older IV_WRX_ANALYSIS report was already a big step forward: it automated the WRX clearing calculation and showed that the system was simply following its own rules.

But that approach had some important limits:

  • It focused on WRX only (GR/IR account line).

  • It worked mainly in 1st local currency.
  • It concentrated on the final calculation, not on the whole story behind the document.

At some point I realized: for the kind of questions we see today, “WRX only” is not enough.

What consultants actually ask us

Here is how MM invoice issues usually arrive in Support:

  • “GRIR quantity is equal but PO is open due to currency conversion.”

  • “GRs and IRs imbalance at LC3 level while LC1/LC2 are fine.”
  • “Exchange rate difference between GR and IR is settling to material account; we expected KDM.”
  • “MIRO posting triggers UMB account and we want to know why.”
  • “MR8M: Posting and reversal amount does not match”
  • “New 0‑value line in local currency (LC3) in all VIM invoices after an upgrade.”

Notice what’s going on here:

  • Customers rarely say “Please show me the formula from Note 1674290.”

  • They say “Why is this GR/IR line so strange?” or “Why is this reversal not a mirror?”

All these scenarios have one thing in common:
You can answer them with the existing notes and manual calculations – but only if you’re ready to become a part‑time human calculator.

I wanted something better for consultants: a way to get the same level of explanation without having to rebuild all equations every time.

What I decided to build instead

Therefore, it is not enough to show to the consultants how WRX was calculated. It would be much better to access the whole story behind the invoice:

  • Which documents were taken into account.

  • How quantities and values accumulated over time.
  • How the system split the outcome into stock (BSX), price differences (PRD), exchange‑rate differences (KDM), GR/IR clearing (WRX), and sometimes revaluation (UMB) and other lines.
  • And, specially, why the accounting document is following standard design and, even if the amounts look strange, the system knows it did the right thing

So I built a new analysis report with a very simple idea: If you’re going to trust the accounting document, let’s show you exactly what the system saw and how it used it.

Design goals were straightforward:

  1. Show the entire PO/SA history for the invoice item
    Not just a final WRX total, but a line‑by‑line list of relevant GRs and IRs, so it’s obvious which documents the system actually considered.

  2. Support multiple currencies
    Consultants can switch the report to document currency or up to three local currencies, which is crucial when GR/IR appears balanced in one currency but strange in another.
  3. Explain the logic, not just print numbers
    After the history tables, the report has a “brain” section that walks through the main steps of the posting logic – in a way that matches the KBAs, but applied to the specific case you’re analyzing.
  4. Keep the input simple
    On the selection screen you basically tell the report:
    • which invoice item to analyze,
    • which currency type you want to see, and
    • in case of multiple account assignment, which account assignment line.

  5. Make it easy to share and debug
    When a customer sends the report output back to SAP, the structure is friendly both for humans and for any future tooling that needs to understand what happened.

And because the older IV_WRX_ANALYSIS report was already useful, I didn’t throw it away: I used it as a basis for this new solution.

What the new report actually shows

Without going into technical field names, here’s how the output is organized and why it helps.

Part 1: The ingredients

  1. Context block – what are we analyzing?
    At the top you see the invoice number, fiscal year, item, the PO reference and the general context.

  2. Goods Receipt history – “how did we get here on the GR side?”
    The first table lists all relevant GR documents for that PO item, and explains how GR quantities and amounts contribute to the final totals.

  3. Invoice history – “what happened before this invoice?”
    The next table does the same for previous IRs (and subsequent debits/credits), including reversals.
    It shows how much has already been invoiced before the document you’re analyzing.

Part 2: The Recipe

  1. Logic explanation 
    This is the part that replaces pages of manual calculation:

    • It shows how the GR/IR clearing line is derived, so you can see why WRX doesn’t always match the item amount.

    • It explains how the system splits the amount into:

      • Stock postings (BSX),
      • Price differences (PRD),
      • Exchange‑rate differences (KDM),
      • and, when relevant, revaluation (UMB) and other lines such as EIN, EKG, FRE etc.

    You don’t have to know every detail of the formulas from the KBAs – the report is essentially doing those calculations for you and showing the intermediate steps.

Part 3: The results

Final amounts – “what should the FI document look like?”
At the end, the report prints the final calculated amounts per transaction key, in a format that mirrors the accounting document.
This is the part you can compare directly with the FI document. If they match, you’ve just proved that the system is behaving as designed (and you didn’t need a spreadsheet to get there).

Your turn

The report is intentionally not a monster transaction. It’s something you can run quickly whenever a document is questioned and you need to audit results and investigate documents that look wrong.

To keep things simple (and consistent with SAP Support processes), the report ZIV_ACCOUNTING_ANALYSIS is delivered via SAP Note 2427297 – Analysis report to explain IV accounting documents

From there you can:

  • Download the report source from the attachments of the note and implement it manually via SE38 in your system, as described in the note.

    • The report is not available for SNOTE implementation. For that reason, you need to get the attachment for manual implementation
  • Then run it like any other custom analysis tool, using the straightforward selection screen.

The note itself remains the official reference for obtaining and implementing the report; the blog post you’re reading is about why it exists and how it can help you as a consultant.

entry.title: What is a SAP currency conversion?
entry.content:
If you read my previous article on SAP plant reallocation, you will have seen how political, economic and global change and uncertainty drive the requirement for complex technical change in SAP customers' ERP systems. 

Over the past few years, organisations have had to navigate inflation, volatile exchange rates, geopolitical instability and significant economic uncertainty. In some markets, businesses have faced sustained pressure from weakening local currencies. In others, governments have introduced entirely new currencies or announced plans to adopt new ones. Bulgaria’s recent adoption of the Euro is a good example of how these changes can quickly move from political discussion to operational reality.

When these events happen, businesses need to respond quickly. This often creates a very specific SAP challenge. How do you change the currency structure of an SAP system that may have been operating in the same currency for years?

At first glance, this may not sound particularly challenging. SAP already supports multiple currencies and most organisations regularly process transactions in foreign currencies. However, changing the company code currency is a very different challenge altogether. Once a company code has been operating for years, its currency becomes deeply embedded within financial processes, transactional records and reporting structures.

This is where a SAP currency conversion project comes in.

What is SAP currency conversion?
